purpose: Public-facing landing document for the Synapse repository on GitHub. Targets human developers (not agents) — sells the product, lists prerequisites (Node 18+, npm, Claude Code CLI, macOS), walks through clone/install/launch, explains how to point a dashboard at a project, demonstrates `!p_track` usage, and enumerates every feature (live dashboard, chat, IDE, git manager, live preview, PKI, TOC). Roughly 800 lines and uses centered HTML/badges for marketing presentation. This is where new contributors and users start; CLAUDE.md and AGENTS.md are where agents start.
gotchas: Claims `npm start` builds the React UI with Vite into `dist/` and launches Electron — verified accurate against package.json (`vite build && unset ELECTRON_RUN_AS_NODE && electron .`), but the `unset ELECTRON_RUN_AS_NODE` step is undocumented in the README and matters when launching from environments that set this var.

States 'No separate server process needed -- everything runs inside Electron.' This is true for the bundled flow but contradicts start.sh / launch.sh which still check port 3456 — that port is used by the SSE server hosted INSIDE Electron, not a separate process.
README references 47 commands across two categories — keep this count in sync with _commands/Synapse/ (25) and _commands/project/ (22) when commands are added/removed.
Says Synapse 'currently targets macOS' — the .command launchers and `electron-builder --mac` script confirm this; Linux/Windows users will hit unsupported paths.
Marketing badges link to anonymized URLs (`[![Electron]()...]`) — the empty parens are intentional placeholders, not broken links to fix.
patterns: Pattern: 'What changes with Synapse' before/after table appears immediately after the badges to set value framing in <100 words.
Section ordering: Getting Started → Usage Guide → Features → Commands → Best Practices → How It Works (decision-tree first, deep-dive last).
Comparison tables (`!p_track` vs `!p` vs `!master_plan_track`) appear at every decision point so users don't have to memorize differences.
Every prerequisite is paired with a verification command (`node --version`, `npm --version`).
conventions: Uses HTML `<div align="center">` blocks for hero/branding sections that GitHub renders but plain markdown tooling ignores gracefully.
Code fences distinguish shell commands (```bash) from chat input (no language tag) — chat examples use `!command` syntax without a `$` prompt.
Em-dashes `--` (double hyphen) used throughout instead of Unicode em-dash, matching the rest of the repo.
Headings follow GitHub-flavored anchor convention — `## Getting Started` matches the `[Getting Started](#getting-started)` TOC link.
